planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring by carefully separating rhizomes, or transplant container-grown plants into consistently moist, humus-rich, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in part shade; stream orchid grows naturally along seeps, stream banks, and wet canyon floors and requires consistent soil moisture without standing water. It is the most easily cultivated terrestrial orchid native to North America.

directions: Plant in consistently moist, humus-rich, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in part shade; stream orchid is the most garden-amenable of North America's native terrestrial orchids, found naturally along spring-fed seeps, stream banks, and wet canyon floors throughout western North America from British Columbia to Texas. It produces upright stems with broad, pleated, lance-shaped leaves arranged alternately along their length, and in late spring to early summer bears intricate, nodding flowers loosely arranged along the upper stem — each bloom unmistakably orchid-shaped with a cupped, lip petal striped and marked in green, pink, and purple-maroon, like a small, graceful wild orchid spike. Unlike many terrestrial orchids that refuse garden cultivation, E. gigantea establishes well from divisions in consistently moist, partly shaded sites near streams, ponds, or rain gardens. It spreads slowly by rhizomes to form modest colonies over time and is an outstanding plant for a naturally moist shade garden where reliably wet soils support it.

planting.note: Sow seeds on the surface of moist soil in fall for spring germination, or sow in early spring — seeds require light to germinate and must not be covered with soil. Unlike common foxglove which is biennial, Digitalis ferruginea is typically a short-lived perennial persisting 3–5 years in ideal conditions. It self-sows freely to maintain a permanent colony in the garden.

directions: Sow seeds on the surface of moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in part shade to full sun; rusty foxglove is a tall, refined species from southeastern Europe and Turkey producing exceptionally architectural flower spikes — much more densely packed and finely detailed than common foxglove. The flowers are small (3/4 to 1 inch), closely arranged along the spike, and colored in warm, tawny russet-brown to rusty-orange-cream with intricate darker veining and netting visible inside the tube, giving the whole spike a burnished, antiqued quality from which it takes its name. Unlike common foxglove (Digitalis purpurea), which typically dies after flowering, D. ferruginea is a genuine short-lived perennial persisting 3–5 years in favorable conditions while self-sowing freely to maintain a colony. The upright spikes can reach 4–7 feet in excellent conditions and are extraordinarily architectural in the border. Combines beautifully with ornamental grasses, purple-leafed shrubs, and golden-foliaged plants where the warm rusty flower color finds its best complement.

planting.note: Sow seeds on the surface of moist soil without covering — seeds require light to germinate — in early spring or fall; yellow foxglove is considerably more long-lived than common foxglove, persisting as a true perennial for many years in suitable conditions. Divide established clumps in early spring to propagate named selections and rejuvenate older plants.

directions: Sow seeds on the soil surface in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in part shade to full sun; yellow foxglove is the most genuinely long-lived foxglove species, behaving as a true perennial for 5–10 or more years rather than the typical 2-year biennial cycle of common foxglove — a crucial distinction for the perennial border. It produces dense spikes 2–3 feet tall of small, pale-yellow to cream tubular flowers with faint darker veining inside the tube in early summer, and often reblooms on lateral shoots if cut back after the main flowering flush. The foliage is glossy, dark-green, and forms a neat, semi-evergreen clump in mild climates that is attractive throughout the year. More shade-tolerant than most foxgloves, it succeeds in dappled woodland shade under deciduous trees where the common foxglove often fails to bloom well. A refined, dependable, long-lived plant for the shaded border that deserves far wider use than its quieter color gives it.

planting.note: Scarify or nick seeds before sowing in lean, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; yellow wild indigo is extremely slow the first 2–3 years as it develops a deep taproot, then becomes very long-lived and drought-tolerant. Do not move established plants — the deep taproot makes transplanting nearly impossible after the first year. As a nitrogen-fixing legume, it gradually improves soil over time.

directions: Sow scarified seeds in lean, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; yellow wild indigo is a bushy, rounded native legume of eastern North America, native to dry, sandy woodlands, pine barrens, and open ridges from Maine to Florida, producing bright, clear-yellow pea flowers in loose clusters along upright stems from June through August — a different bloom time than the blue (B. australis) and white (B. alba) species. The attractive three-parted blue-green leaves turn black after frost, and the inflated, rattling seed pods are ornamental in fall and winter. As a nitrogen-fixing legume, it gradually improves surrounding soil fertility and thrives in dry, lean soils that challenge most garden plants. It is extremely long-lived once established — plants can persist 50 or more years in appropriate conditions without disturbance. A critical late-summer nectar source for bumblebees and specialist native bees that depend specifically on baptisia flowers for pollen collection.

planting.note: Plant container-grown or balled-and-burlapped trees in moist, fertile, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in spring or fall; katsura requires consistent soil moisture especially during the first 2–3 years, and is prone to leaf scorch in hot, dry conditions. Afternoon shade is beneficial in inland climates with hot summers. In fall, the dropped leaves emit the famous caramel-cotton candy fragrance.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in full sun to part shade; katsura tree is one of the most beautiful ornamental trees for four-season interest, producing small, heart-shaped, scalloped-edged leaves that move through a sequence of striking colors — emerging in spring with bronze-red or purplish new growth, maturing to clean blue-green through summer, then turning in autumn to brilliant shades of yellow, apricot, and pink. As the leaves fall they emit an extraordinary fragrance universally described as caramel, cotton candy, or freshly baked sugar — one of the most enchanting scents in the autumn garden. The tree also develops attractive, finely furrowed, shaggy russet-brown bark on older specimens that is ornamental through winter. Native to mountain forests of Japan and China, it grows as a graceful, pyramidal tree that softens with age, often with multiple ascending trunks. A magnificent specimen for a moist, partly shaded position where its four-season sensory qualities can be fully appreciated.
